title: Rajesh Kumar Sharma v. Infosys BPO Limited & Anr.
keyArguments: The termination of the petitioner was effected without following the principles of natural justice as no show cause notice was issued prior to dismissal.
The respondent employer failed to conduct a proper domestic inquiry as mandated under the standing orders applicable to the establishment.
The alleged misconduct of the petitioner was not substantiated by any cogent or reliable evidence placed before the inquiry officer.
The petitioner had an unblemished service record of eleven years and the punishment of dismissal was disproportionate to the alleged misconduct.
The termination letter cited reasons not disclosed to the petitioner during the inquiry proceedings, vitiating the entire disciplinary process.
judgmentSummary: The High Court of Delhi allowed the writ petition filed by the petitioner-employee and set aside the order of termination passed by Infosys BPO Limited. The court held that the domestic inquiry was vitiated by procedural irregularities and violation of principles of natural justice. The petitioner was directed to be reinstated with 50 percent back wages from the date of termination.
judgmentText:
IN THE HIGH COURT OF DELHI AT NEW DELHI
W.P.(C) No. 4418 of 2022

RAJESH KUMAR SHARMA ... Petitioner
Versus
INFOSYS BPO LIMITED & ANR. ... Respondents

Date of Decision: 14th March, 2023

CORAM: HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE SANJIV NARULA

JUDGMENT

1. The present writ petition has been filed under Article 226 of the Constitution of India challenging the order of termination dated 03rd September, 2021 passed by Respondent No. 1, Infosys BPO Limited, against the petitioner, who was serving as a Senior Process Associate.

2. Brief facts as emerging from the record are that the petitioner was employed with the respondent company since the year 2010 and had served without any adverse remark until the year 2021. On 15th July, 2021, the petitioner was placed under suspension on the allegation of sharing confidential client data with an unauthorized third party. A domestic inquiry was purportedly initiated, and an inquiry officer was appointed. However, the petitioner submits, and the record demonstrates, that the inquiry proceedings were conducted in a summary and perfunctory manner without affording the petitioner a meaningful opportunity to cross-examine the witnesses produced against him.

3. Learned counsel for the petitioner, Mr. Avinash Tripathi, submitted that the inquiry officer relied upon electronic records which were not authenticated in accordance with Section 65B of the Indian Evidence Act, 1872, and that the petitioner was denied the assistance of a co-worker of his choice during the domestic inquiry, in violation of the standing orders governing the establishment. It was further urged that the termination letter dated 03rd September, 2021 introduced fresh grounds of misconduct which were never put to the petitioner during the inquiry, thereby causing irreparable prejudice.

4. Learned counsel for the respondent, Ms. Priya Menon, contended that the domestic inquiry was conducted fairly and in accordance with the certified standing orders, that sufficient opportunity was granted to the petitioner, and that the findings of the inquiry officer were based on evidence that was duly proved. It was submitted that the petitioner had himself admitted to accessing restricted files beyond the scope of his authorization.

5. This Court has carefully perused the inquiry record. It is evident that the electronic evidence relied upon by the inquiry officer was not accompanied by a certificate as required under Section 65B of the Indian Evidence Act, 1872, as interpreted by the Supreme Court in Arjun Panditrao Khotkar v. Kailash Kushanrao Gorantyal, (2020) 7 SCC 1. The reliance placed upon such inadmissible evidence is a fundamental infirmity that goes to the root of the inquiry proceedings.

6. Furthermore, this Court finds that the petitioner was not furnished with copies of the documents relied upon by the management prior to the commencement of inquiry proceedings. The right to a fair hearing is not a mere formality but a substantive guarantee rooted in Articles 14 and 21 of the Constitution of India. The denial of this right renders the inquiry proceedings null and void.

7. On the question of proportionality, this Court notes that the petitioner had an eleven-year unblemished service record. Even assuming some degree of procedural lapse on the part of the petitioner, the punishment of dismissal is shockingly disproportionate to the alleged act and cannot be sustained in law.

8. In view of the foregoing, the writ petition is allowed. The order of termination dated 03rd September, 2021 is hereby quashed and set aside. The petitioner shall be reinstated in service within four weeks from the date of this order. The petitioner shall be entitled to 50 percent of back wages for the period of his illegal termination. All other consequential service benefits shall follow.

9. Parties to bear their own costs.

(SANJIV NARULA)
JUDGE
MARCH 14, 2023
